rulings.Case Title: TRENT JASON VS. AMERICAN AUTOMOBILE ASSOCIATION OF NORTHERN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions For Bad Faith Actions Or Tactics Pursuant To Civ. Proc. Section 128.5
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 24, 2019, Line 3, Defendant American Automobile Association of Northern California, Nevada & Utah's motion for sanctions for bad faith actions or tactics is denied. Defendant asserts that whether Plaintiff has engaged in "bad-faith actions or tactics that are frivolous or solely intended to cause unnecessary delay" (Code Civ. Proc. § 128.5(a)) is "measured by the objective reasonable attorney standard." (Mot. at 6.) Defendant is wrong. Section 128.5 was amended in 2017 to specifically overrule San Diegans for Open Government v. City of San Diego (2016) 247 Cal.App.4th 1306, which held that "an objective standard applies when determining whether a party's or an attorney's conduct is sanctionable under section 128.5." (In re Marriage of Sahafzadeh-Taeb and Taeb (2019) 39 Cal.App.5th 124, 128.) That legislation clarified that " 'the standard applied in Section 128.5 is subjective bad faith.'" (Id. at 134.) Thus, the 2017 legislation returns the meaning of "bad faith" under section 128.5 to the majority view prior to San Diegans, which " 'require[d] a showing not only of a meritless or frivolous action or tactics, but also of bad faith in taking the action or tactic.'" (Id. at 133-135.) In addition to relying on the wrong legal standard, Defendant also asserts that Plaintiff should be sanctioned because he has appealed from the Court's ruling granting its motion for summary judgment. (Mot. at 2, 4, 6.) Defendant urges the Court to grant its motion "to prevent further abusive tactics directed toward both defendant and the legal system." (Id. at 6.) That request is improper. The Court will not award sanctions to deter a party from filing an appeal that it has a right to prosecute, and the merits of which the Court will not (and cannot) prejudge. If Defendant believes that Plaintiff's appeal is frivolous, it may seek appropriate relief from the Court of Appeal. (See In re Marriage of Flaherty (1982) 31 Cal.3d 637, 650-651.) Defendant is correct in asserting that "[w]hen a tactic or action utterly lacks merit, a court is entitled to infer the party knew it lacked merit yet pursued the action for some ulterior motive." (Dolan v. Buena Engineers, Inc. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 1500, 1505.) "But it also 'is within a court's discretion not to draw that inference if convinced the party was acting in the good faith belief the action was meritorious.'" (In re Marriage of Sahafzadeh-Taeb and Taeb, 39 Cal.App.5th at 144, quoting Dolan, 24 Cal.App.4th at 144.) Here, Defendant "points to no evidence which conclusively demonstrates [Plaintiff] pursued [his] action against it in bad faith." (Dolan, 24 Cal.App.4th at 1505.) The Court granted summary judgment for Defendant based on a finding that Plaintiff failed to present any evidence to support his claims, and rejected his various theories as unsupported by controlling law. Nevertheless, it cannot conclude that Plaintiff necessarily acted in subjective bad faith, however misguided or even foolish his theories may be. Nor does it see any basis for ordering a self-represented litigant to pay a $24,000+ sanctions award, which can have no practical benefit and will only multiply the litigation that Defendant claims it wishes to avoid. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling, and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GEORGIA HERDENER VS. TAPJOY, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Plaintiffs And Non-Party Witness Paul Bowens Motion To Seal Documents Filed In Support Of Plaintiffs Opposition To Defendants Motion For Summary Judgment, Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 24, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFF GEORGIA HERDENER And Non-Party Witness Paul Bowens' Motion To Seal Documents Filed In Support Of Plaintiffs Opposition To Defendants Motion For Summary Judgment, Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Granted. No opposition filed and the motion complies with California Rules of Court, Rules 2.550 and 2.551. The Court finds that there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the portions of the records to be sealed; the overriding interest supports sealing those records; a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. rulings.Case Title: CRYSTAL LEI VS. DEMAS YAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Cyrstal Lei And Bryant Fu'S Motion For Order Setting Attorneys Fees In Favor Of Plaintiffs
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 24, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFFS CRYSTAL LEI, BRYANT FU'S Motion For Order Setting Attorneys Fees In Favor Of Plaintiffs Plaintiffs Crystal Lei and Bryant Fu's motion for an order setting attorneys fees in favor of Plaintiffs and against Defendant Demas Yan is granted in the amount of $42,687.69. Plaintiffs prevailed on Yan's anti-SLAPP special motion to strike, and this Court awarded attorneys' fees on the ground that the special motion was frivolous. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 425.16(c).) The Court of Appeal affirmed that order in its entirety, finding that Yan's anti-SLAPP motion was a bad faith delay tactic. "A statute authorizing an attorney fee award at the trial court level includes appellate attorney fees unless the statute specifically provides otherwise." (Evans v. Unkow (1995) 38 Cal.App.4th 1490, 1499.) "Section 425.16, subdivision (c), does not preclude recovery of appellate attorney fees; hence attorney fees recoverable under the statute include appellate fees." (Carpenter v. Jack in the Box Corp. (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 454, 461.) Thus, "[t]he trial court's authority to award fees and costs under section 425.16, subdivision (c), includes authority to award fees incurred in responding to an appeal of an order granting or denying a special motion to strike, or of an order awarding attorney fees in connection with such motion." (Id.; see also, e.g., Baharian-Mehr v. Smith (2010) 189 Cal.App.4th 265, 275 [plaintiff's attorney fees on appeal from order finding anti-SLAPP motion frivolous are "recoverable under the statute"]; Taxpayer and Consumer Rights v. Garamendi (2005) 132 Cal.App.4th 1375, 1395 [same].) Yan shall pay the fees awarded within 10 days of entry of the Court's order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CRYSTAL LEI VS. DEMAS YAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs' Motion To Tax And Strike Costs
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 24, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFFS CRYSTAL LEI, BRYANT FU'S Motion To Tax And Strike Costs Plaintiffs Crystal Lei and Bryant Fu's motion to tax and strike costs is denied. In its August 14, 2019 Statement of Decision and Judgment, the Court directed that judgment be entered in favor of Defendants Tina Yan, Cheuk Tin Yan, and 547 Investments, LLC. As such, those parties are prevailing defendants entitled to recover their statutory costs as a matter of right. (Code Civ. Proc. § 1032.) Plaintiffs have not met their burden to show that the costs claimed were not actually or reasonably incurred. (See Ladas v. California State Auto. Assn. (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 761, 774 ["If the items appearing in a cost bill appear to be proper charges, the burden is on the party seeking to tax costs to show that they were not reasonable or necessary."].) A claim of entitlement to an "offset" is not a proper basis for a motion to strike or tax costs. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: FIVE STARS INVESTMENTS LLC VS. THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order Regarding Defendants Supplemental Expert Disclosure And Defendants Expert Micheal Flemming And For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday October 24, 2019 Line 2 - PLAINTIFF FIVE STARS INVESTMENTS LLC's Motion For Protective Order Regarding Defendants Supplemental Expert Disclosure And Defendants Expert Micheal Flemming And For Monetary Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE to reassertion at trial. Without an expert report or deposition testimony, the Court cannot determine whether Defendant's expert Mr. Flemming's anticipated testimony will address only "subject[s] to be covered by" Plaintiff's expert Mr. Colton, which would be permitted by CCP 2034.280, or would address subjects not covered by Mr. Colton, which would not. Defendant must make Mr. Flemming available for deposition before Mr. Colton's trial testimony so that the trial judge may consider, based on a properly developed record, any renewed motion to preclude Mr. Flemming from testifying, or to limit his testimony. The requests for sanctions by both parties are DENIED. Plaintiff's request is denied because the motion is denied (albeit without prejudice). Defendant's request is denied because Plaintiff's request to make Mr. Flemming available for deposition is granted. . Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. = (JPT/D302)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN FLORES ET AL VS. DOE 1 THROUGH 11 ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees After Special Motion To Strike Strategic Lawsuit Against Public Participation
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 24, 2019, Line 7, PLAINTIFFS JOHN FLORES, PATRICE FLORES, BRITELITE IMMERSIVE INC.'S Motion For Attorneys Fees After Special Motion To Strike Strategic Lawsuit Against Public Participation Cross-Defendants' motion for attorneys' fees is granted in the amount of $46,504.00 in attorneys' fees and $371.00 in costs. Cross-Defendants are entitled to a mandatory award of attorneys' fees in connection with their successful anti-SLAPP motion. (Code Civ. Proc. § 425.16(c).) Cross-Complainants Dennis Wanken and Eleni Wanken baselessly and unprofessionally accuse moving parties of "an interest in inflating the amount claimed" and of "attempts to pad the bill," and assert that because the case was "not complex," an award of $25,000 would be more appropriate. The Court, having reviewed counsel's briefing and argument in connection with the special motion to strike, disagrees. The Court finds that counsel's hourly rates are reasonable and that the overall amount of time spent by counsel is reasonable in light of the factual and legal issues presented by the motion and the cross-complaint. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for cross-defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANDREW EZEKIEL HERMAN VS. THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 24, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANT CITY OF BRISBANE MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Defendant City of Brisbane's motion for summary judgment is granted. The injury occurred on a paved bike path in the Candlestick Point Recreation State Area. (Complaint, 9-11, 14; Ceizler Decl., Ex. G.) The recreational path is part of the Bay Trail. (Breault Dec., 9-11.) Plaintiff's claim fails because Brisbane is entitled to trail immunity under Gov't Code § 831.4(b). The undisputed evidence shows that the injury occurred because a condition of the trail, i.e., a gap in the concrete, caused plaintiff to fall while he was using the trail to access a recreational or scenic area. (See pictures attached to Plaintiff's Exs. B and D.) Under the circumstances, the City is entitled to absolute immunity under section 831.4(b). (See, e.g., Buergueno v. Regents of University of California (2015) 243 Cal.App.4th 1052 [affirming summary judgment for bicycle injury on paved bikeway]; Farnham v. City of Los Angeles (1998) 68 Cal.App.4th1097 [granting judgment on the pleadings based on trail immunity where portion of paved bikeway gave way and caused plaintiff to be thrown into ditch]; Carroll v. County of Los Angles (1997) 60 Cal.App.4th 606 [trail immunity applied to paved bike path with a crack that caused rollerblader to be injured]; Armenio v. County of San Mateo (1994) 28 Cal.App.4th 413 [trail immunity barred biker's claim where improper patching of the paved trail caused the injury].) Plaintiff's reliance on Toeppe v. City of San Diego (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 921 is misplaced. There, the court held that trail immunity did not apply where a branch from a eucalyptus tree that was a man-made addition fell on plaintiff. The court explained that the tree constituted a dangerous condition regardless of the location of the subject trail. "In short, this is not a case about trails. It is about trees. Trees that were planted and maintained by the City. Trees that were not naturally occurring in Mission Bay Park." (Toeppe, 13 Cal.App.5th at 931.) Here, plaintiff is seeking to recover based on the condition of the trail, which included the utility box. The dangerous condition (gap in the pavement) cannot be divorced from the trail and therefore, Toeppe is inapposite. The court denies the City of Brisbane's request for an award of attorneys' fees per Code of Civ. Proc., § 1038. Brisbane has not presented any evidence of any costs or attorneys' fees it incurred. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Brisbane is required to submit a proposed order for signature and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANDREW EZEKIEL HERMAN VS. THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Joinder
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 24, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S JOINDER TO MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant City and County of San Francisco's joinder to Brisbane's motion for summary judgment is denied. The City failed to provide sufficient notice per Code of Civ. Proc., § 437c(a)(2) and neglected to file a separate statement of undisputed facts. (See Village Nurseries, L.P. v. Greenbaum (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 26, 46-47 [joinder to motion for summary judgment cannot be granted where joining party fails to file its own separate statement of undisputed facts]; Frazee v. Seely (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 627, 636 [same].) In view of the Court's ruling on the City of Brisbane's motion, however, which raises identical issues, Plaintiff would be well-advised to stipulate to judgment in favor of the City and County of San Francisco, or to dismiss the complaint as to it. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: RACHEL GOLDBLATT ET AL VS. PRIORITY BICYCLES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 24, 2019, Line 12 DEFENDANT VELOFIX HOLDINGS USA, INC. DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendant Velofix Holdings USA, Inc.'s demurrer to the amended complaint is overruled in its entirety. Plaintiff alleges sufficient facts showing that defendant could be liable based on an agency relationship with Velofix Bay Area, which is alleged to have distributed or sold the bicycle. (Amended Complaint, 6-10, 17; see Arriaga v. CitiCapital Commercial Corp. (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 1527, 1534 ["Beyond manufacturers, anyone identifiable as 'an integral part of the overall producing and marketing enterprise' is subject to strict liability"]; Bay Summit Community Assn. v. Shell Oil Co. (1996) 51 Cal.App.4th 762, 772-773 [parties subject to strict liability include manufacturers, retailers, others involved in the vertical distribution of consumer goods including wholesale and retail distributors and licensors, and nonmanufacturing parties outside the vertical chain of distribution of a product which profit from placing the product into the stream of commerce]; Cislaw v. Southerland Corp. (1992) 4 Cal.App.4th 1284, 1289 [franchise agreement may give rise to agency relationship].) In addition, the amended complaint alleges that defendant sold the bicycle at issue to the Fairmont Hotel. (Amended Complaint, 17.) Such alternative pleading is permissible. (See Adams v. Paul (1995) 11 Cal.4th 583, 593 ["a party may plead in the alternative and may make inconsistent allegations"].) Discovery will resolve the precise details of the bicycle transaction, and there remain factual issues regarding whether the Fairmont Hotel purchased the bicycle directly from defendant's website and what precise role defendant played in its distribution or sale, which could support the strict liability and negligence claims. (See Bay Summit Community Assn., 51 Cal.App.4th at 774 ["In applying this stream of commerce theory, the courts have eschewed legal labels and have taken a very practical approach, focusing on the actual connection between the defendant's activities and the defective product."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A. VS. JOONG Y IM
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Deem Requests For Admissions Admitted And Of Nonappearance
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday October 24, 2019 Line 1 -PLAINTIFF WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'s Motion To Deem Requests For Admissions Admitted And Of Nonappearance. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to deem admitted is GRANTED. No timely opposition filed. CCP 2033.280 Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)
